nodes.name: Pulitzer Prize Fiction
nodes.description: Notable Pulitzer Prize for Fiction winners

exercises.name: Pulitzer Prize for Fiction — Notable Winners

items.prompt: This 1939 Steinbeck novel about the Joad family's Dust Bowl migration to California won the Pulitzer in 1940.
items.explanation:
John Steinbeck, Pulitzer 1940; Steinbeck cited it in his 1962 Nobel Prize acceptance speech
Based on Steinbeck's 1936 San Francisco News articles on migrant farm workers
Banned and burned in several California counties as 'communist propaganda'
Title from 'The Battle Hymn of the Republic': 'the vintage where the grapes of wrath are stored'
John Ford directed the acclaimed 1940 film adaptation with Henry Fonda

items.prompt: This 1946 Robert Penn Warren novel follows the rise and fall of Willie Stark, a fictional Southern populist governor modeled on Huey Long. Won the Pulitzer in 1947.
items.explanation:
Robert Penn Warren, Pulitzer 1947; Warren is the only person to win Pulitzers for both fiction and poetry
Willie Stark is widely read as a roman à clef for Louisiana Governor Huey Long, assassinated in 1935
Narrated by Jack Burden, a political aide whose own moral compromise mirrors Stark's rise
Title alludes to the nursery rhyme 'Humpty Dumpty' — all the king's men cannot put him back together
Adapted for film in 1949 (Best Picture Oscar) and again in 2006

items.prompt: This 1960 Harper Lee novel set in Depression-era Alabama, narrated by Scout Finch, won the Pulitzer in 1961.
items.explanation:
Harper Lee, Pulitzer 1961; has sold over 40 million copies and is a staple of American high school curricula
Atticus Finch modeled partly on Lee's father; childhood neighbor Truman Capote appears as Dill
Lee published no other novel for 55 years until *Go Set a Watchman* (2015)
Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2007
Gregory Peck won the Academy Award for Best Actor for the 1962 film adaptation

items.prompt: This 1979 Norman Mailer 'true life novel' about the execution of murderer Gary Gilmore won the Pulitzer in 1980.
items.explanation:
Norman Mailer, Pulitzer 1980; based on the real case of Gary Gilmore, executed by firing squad in Utah in 1977
Gilmore was the first person executed in the U.S. after the Supreme Court reinstated the death penalty in 1976
Nearly 1,100 pages; Mailer called it a 'true life novel' blending journalism and fiction
Won the Pulitzer for Fiction despite extensive use of real events, documents, and interviews
Tommy Lee Jones played Gilmore in the 1982 TV film adaptation; won an Emmy

items.prompt: This posthumously published novel about the pompous New Orleans medievalist Ignatius J. Reilly won the Pulitzer in 1981.
items.explanation:
John Kennedy Toole, Pulitzer 1981; Toole died by suicide in 1969 at age 31 before it was published
His mother Thelma Toole spent 11 years finding a publisher; Louisiana State University Press finally accepted it
One of only two Pulitzer winners published posthumously (the other: *Andersonville*, 1955)
Title from Jonathan Swift: 'When a true genius appears, you may know him by this sign: all the dunces are in confederacy against him'
Widely considered the funniest American novel

items.prompt: This 1982 Alice Walker epistolary novel about a Black woman named Celie in the rural South won the Pulitzer in 1983.
items.explanation:
Alice Walker, Pulitzer 1983 and National Book Award 1983; Walker was the first Black woman to win the Pulitzer for Fiction
Told entirely through letters — to God, then to Celie's sister Nettie in Africa
Spielberg's 1985 film adaptation starred Whoopi Goldberg as Celie and was nominated for 11 Academy Awards, winning none
Walker was also a central figure in the rediscovery of Zora Neale Hurston's work
Adapted as a Broadway musical in 2005; revised revival won Best Musical Revival Tony in 2016

items.prompt: This 1987 Toni Morrison novel about an enslaved woman haunted by her dead daughter in post-Civil War Ohio won the Pulitzer in 1988.
items.explanation:
Toni Morrison, Pulitzer 1988; Morrison won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1993 — first African American woman to do so
Inspired by the true case of Margaret Garner, who killed her daughter rather than allow recapture into slavery
In a 2006 *New York Times* survey, named the best American novel of the past 25 years
Dedicated to 'Sixty Million and more' — those who died in the Middle Passage
Oprah Winfrey produced and starred in the 1998 film adaptation

items.prompt: This 1985 Larry McMurtry epic about a Texas cattle drive to Montana won the Pulitzer in 1986.
items.explanation:
Larry McMurtry, Pulitzer 1986; a 843-page Western epic that revitalized the genre as serious literature
Follows retired Texas Rangers Gus McCrae and Woodrow Call on a cattle drive from the Rio Grande to Montana
Adapted as a four-part CBS miniseries in 1989 starring Robert Duvall and Tommy Lee Jones — won 7 Emmy Awards
McMurtry wrote three sequels and two prequels set in the same world
McMurtry later won the Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay for *Brokeback Mountain* (2006)

items.prompt: This 1993 Annie Proulx novel about a hapless journalist who moves to Newfoundland won the Pulitzer in 1994.
items.explanation:
Annie Proulx, Pulitzer 1994 and National Book Award 1993; Proulx was 57 when it was published
Follows Quoyle, a failure as a journalist and father, who rebuilds his life in a bleak Newfoundland fishing community
Each chapter opens with a knot type from an old seamanship manual — a structural motif throughout
Proulx also wrote the short story 'Brokeback Mountain' (1997), adapted into the Oscar-winning 2005 film
The 2001 film adaptation starred Kevin Spacey, Cate Blanchett, and Judi Dench

items.prompt: This 1997 Philip Roth novel about a prosperous Newark businessman whose daughter becomes a radical bomber won the Pulitzer in 1998.
items.explanation:
Philip Roth, Pulitzer 1998; the first of Roth's 'American Trilogy' alongside *I Married a Communist* and *The Human Stain*
Hero Seymour 'Swede' Levov watches the American Dream collapse when his daughter Merry bombs a post office
Narrated by recurring Roth character Nathan Zuckerman, who reconstructs Swede's life from fragments
Roth won the National Book Award, PEN/Faulkner Award, and Man Booker International Prize during his career
Retired from fiction writing in 2010, explicitly, calling it 'enough'

items.prompt: This 2000 Michael Chabon novel about two cousins who create a comic book superhero during WWII won the Pulitzer in 2001.
items.answer: The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier & Clay
alternates: Amazing Adventures of Kavalier and Clay
items.explanation:
Michael Chabon, Pulitzer 2001; draws heavily on the real history of early comic book creators, many of them Jewish immigrants
Joe Kavalier flees Nazi Prague; his cousin Sam Clay is in Brooklyn; together they create 'The Escapist'
Houdini-inspired escape artistry runs throughout — physical escape as metaphor for Jewish survival in Europe
Characters inspired partly by Jack Kirby and Joe Simon, creators of Captain America
Chabon also wrote *The Adventures of Kavalier & Clay* companion piece *The Escapist* comics

links.text: The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier & Clay

items.prompt: This 2007 Junot Díaz novel about a nerdy Dominican-American and the 'fukú' curse on his family won the Pulitzer in 2008.
items.answer: The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
alternates: Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
items.explanation:
Junot Díaz, Pulitzer 2008 and National Book Critics Circle Award; Díaz's debut novel after the story collection *Drown* (1996)
Blends Spanish slang, Spanglish, footnotes, and sci-fi/fantasy references throughout the narrative
'Fukú americanus' — the Dominican curse tied to Columbus and the Trujillo dictatorship — drives the novel's structure
Oscar is a 'ghetto nerd' obsessed with Tolkien and superhero comics; the novel celebrates outsider passion
Rafael Trujillo's 31-year dictatorship (1930–1961) is the historical horror beneath the family saga
items.cardFront: *The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao*

links.text: The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
*The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao*

items.prompt: This 2008 Elizabeth Strout linked story collection centered on a caustic Maine schoolteacher won the Pulitzer in 2009.
items.explanation:
Elizabeth Strout, Pulitzer 2009; technically a linked story collection structured to read as a novel
Olive Kitteridge is a retired math teacher in the fictional Maine town of Crosby — difficult, perceptive, unsentimental
Each story centers on a different Crosby resident; Olive appears in each, sometimes peripherally, sometimes centrally
Adapted as an HBO miniseries in 2014 starring Frances McDormand; won 8 Emmy Awards including Outstanding Limited Series
Strout published a sequel, *Olive, Again*, in 2019

items.prompt: This 2006 Cormac McCarthy post-apocalyptic novel about a father and son walking south through a devastated America won the Pulitzer in 2007.
items.explanation:
Cormac McCarthy, Pulitzer 2007; also an Oprah's Book Club selection — unusual for McCarthy, whose fiction is notoriously bleak
The nameless father and son are never named; their relationship is the entire emotional universe of the novel
Prose stripped of quotation marks and most punctuation — McCarthy's signature austere style pushed to its extreme
Adapted as a 2009 film starring Viggo Mortensen
McCarthy also wrote *Blood Meridian* (1985), *No Country for Old Men* (2005), and *All the Pretty Horses* (1992)

items.prompt: This 2013 Donna Tartt novel about a boy who survives a museum bombing and steals a Dutch Golden Age painting won the Pulitzer in 2014.
items.explanation:
Donna Tartt, Pulitzer 2014; Tartt's third novel after *The Secret History* (1992) and *The Little Friend* (2002)
Central to the plot is Carel Fabritius's 1654 painting *The Goldfinch*, a tiny masterpiece at the Mauritshuis in The Hague
Theo Decker steals the painting after a bombing kills his mother; the painting becomes his secret talisman for 13 years
Tartt takes approximately 10 years per novel — *The Goldfinch* appeared 11 years after *The Little Friend*
The 2019 film adaptation with Ansel Elgort received poor reviews despite the novel's acclaim
